era_context: The late 17th century: European powers consolidating colonial empires; Louis XIV at the height of French power; the Ottoman Empire declining after the failed siege of Vienna (1683); the Qing Dynasty under Kangxi consolidating control over China; the Mughal Empire in India under Aurangzeb entering its final decades.

events.title: The Glorious Revolution
events.description: In November 1688, Protestant nobles and politicians in England invited William III of Orange, Stadtholder of the Dutch Republic and husband of James II's daughter Mary, to invade England and depose the Catholic King James II. William landed at Torbay on 5 November with a Dutch army. James II fled to France without significant military resistance. William and Mary were proclaimed joint monarchs in February 1689. The revolution was bloodless in England but had profound consequences: it established parliamentary supremacy, secured Protestant succession, and aligned England with the Dutch against France. It also triggered the Williamite War in Ireland (1688–1691) and the Jacobite risings in Scotland.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts by eyewitnesses and participants
English state papers and diplomatic correspondence

events.certainty_note: The Glorious Revolution is one of the most thoroughly documented events of the 17th century, with extensive contemporary records from multiple nations.

events.title: War of the League of Augsburg Begins
events.region: Europe (primarily France, the Rhineland, and the Low Countries)
events.description: In September 1688, Louis XIV of France launched military campaigns in the Rhineland and the Palatinate, initiating the War of the League of Augsburg (also called the Nine Years' War). The war was fought between France and a coalition of European powers (the League of Augsburg, including the Holy Roman Empire, Spain, the Palatinate, and later England and the Dutch Republic) over French territorial expansion and hegemony. The war lasted until 1697 and was one of the major conflicts of the late 17th century, involving campaigns across Europe and colonial theaters.
key_figures: William III (Dutch Stadtholder, later English king)
events.certainty_note: The outbreak of the war is well-documented in diplomatic and military records from multiple European powers.
cross_references: 1697-001 (Treaty of Ryswick, end of the war)

events.title: Aurangzeb's Deccan Campaign Continues
events.description: The Mughal Emperor Aurangzeb continued his long military campaign in the Deccan, fighting against the Maratha Confederacy and other regional powers. By 1688, Aurangzeb was in his late 80s but remained actively engaged in military affairs. The Deccan campaign, which had begun in the 1680s, was costly and ultimately unsuccessful in permanently subduing the Marathas. The campaign drained Mughal resources and contributed to the empire's decline after Aurangzeb's death in 1707.

sources.name: Mughal court records and official histories

events.certainty_note: The continuation of the Deccan campaign is documented in Mughal records, though specific events and dates within the campaign are sometimes uncertain.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor Consolidates Control After Three Feudatories Rebellion
events.description: By 1688, the Kangxi Emperor had fully consolidated Qing control over China following the suppression of the Three Feudatories Rebellion (1673–1681). The rebellion had been a major challenge to Qing authority, but its defeat established the Kangxi Emperor as the supreme authority and allowed him to pursue expansionist policies in Manchuria, Mongolia, and Central Asia. The consolidation of power enabled the Kangxi Emperor to undertake his famous southern inspection tours and to pursue cultural and intellectual achievements.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and court diaries

events.certainty_note: The consolidation of Kangxi's power is well-documented in Qing imperial archives.

events.title: English East India Company Receives New Charter from William III
events.description: Following William III's accession to the English throne, the East India Company received a new charter in 1688 that expanded its powers and privileges. The charter granted the company greater monopoly rights over English trade in Asia and authorized it to establish fortified settlements and maintain military forces. This charter was instrumental in enabling the company's expansion in India, particularly in Bengal, where it would establish the Calcutta factory in 1689.
key_figures: East India Company directors and officials
events.certainty_note: The charter is a primary document with precise dating.

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian War (Morean War) Continues
events.description: The Morean War (1684–1699) between the Ottoman Empire and Venice, with support from the Holy Roman Empire and Papal States, continued in 1688. The war involved campaigns in the Peloponnese, Crete, and the Aegean. Venice achieved significant territorial gains, particularly in the Peloponnese, though the war was costly for both sides. The conflict reflected the Ottoman Empire's declining military capacity and the rise of European naval power in the Mediterranean.
key_figures: Ottoman Sultan Mehmed IV (until 1687), then Süleyman II

sources.name: Venetian state papers and military records

events.certainty_note: The Morean War is well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.
cross_references: 1699-001 (Treaty of Karlowitz, end of the war)

events.title: Jesuit Missions in China Face Increasing Pressure
events.description: By 1688, Jesuit missionaries in China, who had enjoyed considerable influence at the Kangxi court, began to face increasing pressure from Confucian scholars and officials who viewed Christianity as a foreign threat to Chinese traditions. The Kangxi Emperor, while initially favorable to the Jesuits, became more cautious about their influence. This tension would eventually lead to the Chinese Rites Controversy and restrictions on Christian missionary activity in the early 18th century.
events.certainty_note: The increasing pressure on Jesuits is documented in mission records and imperial correspondence, though the exact timeline of escalation is somewhat uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: The Glorious Revolution is often portrayed as a triumph of constitutional liberty and parliamentary rights, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was primarily a power struggle among elites, with limited immediate impact on the lives of ordinary people. The 'bloodless' characterization applies only to England; the revolution triggered violent conflicts in Ireland and Scotland. Additionally, the notion that the revolution established 'religious freedom' is misleading—the Toleration Act of 1689 excluded Catholics and non-Christian minorities, and religious conformity remained legally enforced. The East India Company's charter of 1688 is sometimes presented as a moment of commercial expansion, but it also formalized monopolistic practices that would later be criticized as economically inefficient.
historiographic_note: 1688 is exceptionally well-documented in European sources, particularly English, French, Dutch, and German records. The Glorious Revolution dominates the historical narrative, reflecting the bias of English-language historiography toward English political events. Asian sources (Chinese, Ottoman, Indian) are also available but are often underutilized in Western histories. The perspective is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; the experiences of common people, women, and non-European populations are largely absent. Colonial and missionary perspectives dominate accounts of non-European regions.

graph_edges.note: The Glorious Revolution led directly to the Battle of the Boyne as James II sought to reclaim his throne in Ireland
The Glorious Revolution led to the passage of the Bill of Rights and Toleration Act in 1689
The War of the League of Augsburg lasted until 1697, when the Treaty of Ryswick was signed
The new charter granted to the East India Company in 1688 enabled the establishment of the Calcutta factory in 1689
